pr.title: Add forms list tool to the LLM assistant
pr.author: andrewklaviyo

narrative: Adds a new forms_list tool that lets the LLM assistant query the upstream FormsService on behalf of a user. Most of the work is in the tool shim (forms.py) — argument validation, filter-building, response shaping, error mapping to the SSE protocol — and a tight protocol-based mock so tests can exercise the seam without spinning up the real service. The PR also adds a stub mode so the assistant works in dev environments where FormsService isn't reachable.

chunks.title: Schema & DTOs
chunks.intro: New FormFilters, FormCursor, and FormListItem types — the contract the tool will sit on top of.
chunks.diff:
@@ -10,5 +10,18 @@ from dataclasses import dataclass
 from enum import Enum
 
 
+class FormStatus(Enum):
+    LIVE = "live"
+    DRAFT = "draft"
+    ARCHIVED = "archived"
+
+
 @dataclass(kw_only=True, frozen=True)
 class FormFilters:
     status: list[FormStatus] | None = None
+    channel_type: ChannelType | None = None
+    name_substring: str | None = None  # case-insensitive substring match
@@ -42,2 +55,8 @@ class FormCursor:
     opaque_token: str
+    # Note: no version prefix — if pagination format changes we'll need a migration.

findings.summary: Cursor encoding doesn't pin a schema version
findings.body: The cursor field is an opaque string with no version prefix. If we ever change the pagination format — add a new field, switch encoding, etc. — there's no way to detect old cursors gracefully. Clients would silently get wrong-window results.
findings.evidence: list_dto.py:55-56 defines cursor as opaque str; comment acknowledges the gap but doesn't address it. No version detection in the consumer code (forms.py:_resolve_cursor) either.

strengths: FormFilters uses dataclass(kw_only=True, frozen=True) so positional misuse and accidental mutation are both impossible.
FormStatus enum values match the upstream service strings, no translation layer needed.

chunks.title: The forms list tool
chunks.intro: The shim. Maps tool args to FormFilters, calls the service, shapes the response. Small but load-bearing — the single seam between the LLM's free-form intent and the typed service contract.

findings.summary: Docstring claim isn't pinned by a test
findings.body: The tool docstring claims name_substring does case-insensitive substring matching on the form name, but that semantics is owned entirely by the upstream FormsService and isn't pinned by a test here. If the service changes matching behavior, this docstring silently lies to the LLM.
findings.evidence: forms.py:244-245 promise the case-insensitive behavior; confirmed only as a DTO field comment in forms_service/src/dtos/list_dto.py:29. No end-to-end assertion ties this docstring to actual matching behavior.

findings.body: Existing comment from @andrewklaviyo: "Confirming this default behavior with Tyler in the ticket." If Tyler comes back with "no filter should mean all statuses," the LIVE default flips. Stub mode masks this locally because _StubFormsService ignores filters entirely.

ChannelType is exported from klaviyo.forms.forms_service.__all__ and is the type of FormListItem.channel_type, but it's not imported in forms.py. Works at runtime via attribute access, but the import block is asymmetric with FormStatus / FormType. Add it for consistency.
findings.evidence: forms.py:19-26 imports FormCursor/FormFilters/FormListItem/FormSearchResponse/FormStatus/FormType but not ChannelType; _shape_form uses form.channel_type.value; forms_service/__init__.py:4 exposes ChannelType in __all__.

findings.body: No test covers limit=True / limit=False. The isinstance(limit, bool) guard exists because bool is a subclass of int; without a test, that guard is dark code. The LLM passing True instead of 1 is a realistic input.
findings.evidence: forms.py:100 explicitly guards bool; TestResolveLimit in test_forms.py covers None/in-range/min/max/zero/negative/above-max but not bool.

strengths: RetryableKlaviyoError before KlaviyoClientError in the except chain is correctly ordered with the diamond-inheritance reason commented.
_resolve_cursor uses exact-equality (set(cursor.keys()) != expected_keys) rather than subset checks, closing the "LLM invents a cursor field" hallucination vector.
SSE tool_call_start / result events paired on every path; emit_tool_error always emits a result event before returning.
FormsClient Protocol structurally types only the one method actually used, keeping the mock surface minimal.
Shape helpers extracted as pure functions so tests can exercise shaping independently of tool wiring.

chunks.title: Tool registration
chunks.intro: Wires forms_list into the tool registry so the LLM can discover and call it.
chunks.diff:
@@ -12,3 +12,9 @@ from .lists import lists_tool_definitions
 from .segments import segments_tool_definitions
+from .forms import forms_tool_definitions
 
 
 ALL_TOOLS = [
     *lists_tool_definitions(),
     *segments_tool_definitions(),
+    *forms_tool_definitions(),
 ]

findings.summary: Registry order affects LLM tool discovery ranking
findings.body: The order of tools in ALL_TOOLS influences which tool the LLM picks first when prompts are ambiguous. forms_list is appended last; if forms queries are common, consider promoting it.
findings.evidence: tools/__init__.py:14-18. The system prompt iterates ALL_TOOLS in order when constructing the tool description block.

chunks.title: Tests
chunks.intro: Coverage for argument resolution, filter building, response shaping, and error paths. Uses a protocol-based mock so the upstream FormsService isn't required.
chunks.diff:
@@ -0,0 +1,80 @@
+from unittest.mock import Mock
+import pytest
+from klaviyo.ai_assistant.tools.forms import FormsTool, FormsClient
+
+
+class TestResolveLimit:
+    def test_none_returns_default(self):
+        tool = FormsTool(client=Mock(spec=FormsClient))
+        assert tool._resolve_limit(None) == DEFAULT_LIMIT
+
+    def test_in_range(self):
+        tool = FormsTool(client=Mock(spec=FormsClient))
+        assert tool._resolve_limit(25) == 25
+
+    def test_above_max_clamps(self):
+        tool = FormsTool(client=Mock(spec=FormsClient))
+        assert tool._resolve_limit(MAX_LIMIT + 100) == MAX_LIMIT

findings.summary: Mock fixture could share setup with real-service tests
findings.body: The Mock(spec=FormsClient) setup is duplicated across every test method. A pytest fixture would DRY it up and make it easier to swap in the real client for integration runs.
findings.evidence: test_forms.py:18 onward instantiates the same mock 12 times. No conftest.py fixture exists for FormsClient.

strengths: Each test method names a single behavior; failures will point at the right thing.
Uses pytest.mark.parametrize for the limit-range cases, keeping the test body small.

chunks.title: Stub service
chunks.intro: _StubFormsService returns canned data so the assistant works in dev environments where the real FormsService isn't reachable.
chunks.diff:
@@ -0,0 +1,30 @@
+class _StubFormsService:
+    """Returns canned data when FORMS_SERVICE_URL is unset."""
+
+    def list_forms(self, filters=None, cursor=None, limit=None):
+        # Ignores filters entirely — returns the same 3 forms regardless.
+        return FormSearchResponse(
+            items=[
+                FormListItem(id="f1", name="Welcome", status=FormStatus.LIVE),
+                FormListItem(id="f2", name="Footer signup", status=FormStatus.LIVE),
+                FormListItem(id="f3", name="Holiday 2025", status=FormStatus.ARCHIVED),
+            ],
+            next_cursor=None,
+        )
strengths: Stub explicitly documents that it ignores filters — saves debugging time when dev results look wrong.

chunks.title: Config
chunks.intro: Feature flag plus a test fixture that flips it on for the test suite.

items: Manual smoke test: asked the assistant "list my live signup forms" and verified the response shape
Stub mode: ran the assistant locally with FORMS_SERVICE_URL unset
